intro: New Zealand holiday parks are the best-value family accommodation option — kitchen facilities, playgrounds, pool access, and a community feel. Here are the best family-friendly holiday parks across NZ, by region.

parks.name: Paihia Beach Holiday Park
parks.best_for: Beach access, Bay of Islands cruises

parks.name: Paihia TOP 10 Holiday Park
parks.best_for: Budget families, central location

parks.best_for: Secluded beach, snorkelling

parks.best_for: Hot Water Beach access, natural hot pools

highlights: 5 min walk to Cathedral Cove track

tips.title: Book early for school holidays
tips.body: The best holiday parks — especially at Hot Water Beach, Kaiteriteri, and Queenstown — book out 3–6 months ahead for school holiday periods. Don't leave it to the last month.

tips.title: TOP 10 membership saves 10%
tips.body: A TOP 10 annual membership costs around $49 and gives 10% off at all 50+ TOP 10 holiday parks. It pays for itself in 2–3 nights and is worth buying before any family NZ road trip.

tips.title: Cabins vs powered sites
tips.body: Most holiday parks offer powered sites (for campervans and tents) and cabins/units. Cabins with bathrooms (called 'tourist units' or 'ensuite cabins') sleep families for $120–$200/night, often much cheaper than motels with better facilities.

tips.title: Kitchen facilities cut your food budget
tips.body: Holiday park camp kitchens let you cook your own meals. A family of 4 can eat well for $30–$50/day vs $120+ for three restaurant meals. Most parks have BBQ areas too — stock up at supermarkets.

tips.title: Arrival time matters
tips.body: Most holiday parks have check-in from 2pm. Arriving late on a busy summer day can mean smaller or less sheltered sites. Call ahead if running late — they often hold your booking but may re-allocate spots.

faqs.q: What is a holiday park in New Zealand?
faqs.a: A New Zealand holiday park (also called a motor camp or campsite) is a multi-option accommodation facility offering powered sites for campervans and tents, basic cabins, and self-contained units — all typically sharing communal kitchen, bathroom, and laundry facilities. Most have playgrounds, BBQ areas, and some have pools. They're the best-value family accommodation in NZ.

faqs.q: Are TOP 10 holiday parks worth it?
faqs.a: Yes — especially for families doing a multi-night NZ road trip. TOP 10 parks are independently assessed and meet consistent quality standards. The TOP 10 membership card ($49/year) gives 10% off all stays at 50+ parks nationwide, paying for itself quickly. Not all great parks are TOP 10 parks, but TOP 10 membership is reliable indicator of quality.

faqs.q: How much does a holiday park cost per night in NZ?
faqs.a: Powered sites for campervans or tents cost $35–$55/night for two adults. Cabins (shared bathrooms) run $80–$130/night. Self-contained tourist units with private bathrooms cost $120–$220/night. School holiday and Christmas/New Year prices are typically 20–40% higher than off-peak.

faqs.q: Can you book NZ holiday parks online?
faqs.a: Yes — most NZ holiday parks can be booked directly on their own website, through TOP 10's website (top10.co.nz), or via Booking.com and Campable. Direct booking is usually cheapest. The TOP 10 website includes all 50+ TOP 10 parks and allows easy searching by region.
